source: Zylos AI — Graceful Degradation Patterns in AI Agent Systems
summary: Research on classifying AI agent subprocess errors into retriable vs. non-retriable categories, with exponential-backoff retry reducing manual intervention by ~70% in agentic pipelines.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly addresses limitation #5: no graceful degradation when CLI subprocesses crash or timeout

summary: Temporal demonstrated checkpoint-based recovery with exponential-backoff retry reducing manual intervention in agentic pipelines. Covers crash recovery and resumption at correct workflow step.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — supports the retry/fault-tolerance pattern for ProcessRunner subprocess management

source: Kim et al. — Scaling Test-Time Compute for Agentic Coding (arXiv:2604.16529)
summary: Introduces Parallel-Distill-Refine (PDR) and Recursive Tournament Voting (RTV) for test-time compute scaling in coding agents. Sequential PDR conditions new rollouts on distilled summaries of prior failed attempts, improving SWE-Bench Verified from 70.9% to 77.6%.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly addresses the amnesia-loop problem in AgentForge's remediation stage; PDR maps to distilled-context injection in re-implementation prompts

source: Sakana AI — RL Conductor / Learning to Orchestrate Agents in Natural Language (arXiv:2512.04388, ICLR 2026)
summary: A 7B RL-trained orchestrator (Conductor) routes subtasks between GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro, achieving SOTA on LiveCodeBench (83.9%) and GPQA-Diamond (87.5%) at ~6x cost reduction. Productized as Sakana Fugu.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — the conductor pattern of routing tasks to appropriately-tiered models maps directly to AgentForge's static model mapping limitation; CLI --model flag makes it feasible without training

source: LangMem SDK — LangGraph Long-Term Memory (LangGraph 0.3.19, April 2026)
summary: Episodic, semantic, and procedural memory primitives for LangGraph agents. Procedural memory allows agents to rewrite their own system instructions based on prior run feedback. Open-source under LangChain umbrella.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — addresses limitation #8 (no agent memory across runs), but tightly coupled to LangChain/LangGraph framework which AgentForge doesn't use; would require significant adapter work

source: Kaptein et al. — Runtime Governance for AI Agents: Policies on Paths (arXiv:2603.16586)
summary: Formalizes compliance policies as functions mapping agent identity, partial execution path, proposed next action, and organizational state to a policy violation probability. Shows that prompt-level instructions and static access control are special cases of this framework.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — AgentForge already has policyEngine.ts with stage gate enforcement; this paper provides formal foundations but would mostly validate existing design rather than enable new features

source: Li et al. — T-STAR: Reason in Chains, Learn in Trees (arXiv:2604.07165)
summary: Tree-structured Self-Taught Agent Rectification framework for multi-turn agent policy optimization. Recovers latent correlated reward structure across trajectories, addressing sparse rewards in multi-step reasoning.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — inspiring for plan repair concepts, but T-STAR requires RL training infrastructure that AgentForge doesn't have; the distillation approach from PDR (HID-106) is more immediately implementable

source: AdaptFlow — Adaptive Workflow Optimization via Meta-Learning (arXiv:2508.08053)
summary: MAML-inspired bi-level optimization for agent workflows: inner loop refines for specific subtasks, outer loop updates shared initialization. Outperforms static templates on QA, code generation, and math reasoning.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low-Medium — AgentForge uses a deterministic state machine (not a learnable workflow), so MAML-style adaptation is architecturally misaligned; too early for current AgentForge stage

summary: Weekly-updated curated collection of 2026 arXiv papers on agent engineering, memory, evaluation, workflows, and autonomous systems. Includes multi-agent recommendation frameworks and RL orchestration papers.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — useful ongoing signal source for emerging patterns; no single paper from this collection was novel enough to select this run beyond what was found via direct searches

source: ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement
summary: Workshop summary covering three layers of RSI: improvement operators, intrinsic diagnostics, and governed adaptation frameworks. Discusses verifiability constraint for reliable self-improvement.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — RSI in this context is about model-level self-improvement, not orchestrator-level; the verifiability constraint is relevant but the AgentForge codebase already uses structured JSON output as a verifiability mechanism

source: Requesty Blog — 5 AI Agent Techniques May 2026 (self-evolving, managed, compiled)
summary: Overview of emerging agent techniques including speculative planning (IdleSpec) — agents using idle time between tool calls to pre-plan next steps. Covers self-evolving agent patterns from May 2026.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — speculative/idle planning is interesting for AgentForge's sequential execution model, but requires streaming subprocess I/O which AgentForge does not currently support (limitation #9); too early

source: NirDiamant/Agent_Memory_Techniques — 30 runnable notebooks on agent memory
summary: Comprehensive tutorial repo covering conversation buffers, vector stores, knowledge graphs, episodic/semantic memory, MemGPT, Mem0, Letta, Zep, Graphiti, and LoCoMo benchmarks. Updated for 2026.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — good reference for future cross-run memory feature; Graphiti (temporal KG) is the most interesting pattern for AgentForge's artifact-first model, but cross-run memory is not yet a priority
